The ex-Gunners captain helped Arsenal to FA Cup glory, and was part of the side that reached the Champions League final in 2006.

"Arsene Wenger is like a second father to me," Fabregas told DAZN Italia.

"Obviously, he worked a lot on transitions, attacking the space, something that is even more important in modern football.

"I learned a lot from him and want my team to do that."

Football.title: Ashley Cole questioned for taking surprise first job in Italian second tier despite ‘earning his stripes’ in England
Football.content:
Ashley Cole questioned for taking surprise first job in Italian second tier despite ‘earning his stripes’ in England

Darren Bent was caught off-guard by Ashley Cole's first managerial role and claimed he'd have been better off at a Championship side.

Cole was appointed manager of Serie B side Cesena until the end of the season on Sunday, although he could secure a prolonged stay in northern Italy should certain criteria be met.

4 Cole has done it all as a player, but can he enjoy success as a manager? Credit: Getty

It marked the 45-year-old's first senior managerial role of his coaching career having cut his teeth as an assistant at Chelsea, Everton, Derby County, Birmingham City and England.



Speaking on talkSPORT Drive, Bent pointed out Cole has 'got the credentials' having learned his trade in England and working with some impressive players.

It's why the Chelsea great's decision to take the managerial plunge in the Italian second tier was all the more perplexing.

Cole 'earned his stripes', but Cesena move still surprising

"It does surprise me that he's gone to a second division team in Italy," Bent said.

"I thought he's got the credentials, certainly with his coaching career.



"He's not one of these who, as soon as they've retired, has jumped into a job. He's almost earned his stripes with coaching roles.

"He and Lee Carsley work quite well together as well."

Bent then proposed a scenario where if he was presented with the Cesena role or taking over Championship outfit Hull City, who sit fifth in the second tier, the former Premier League marksman wouldn't look too far away from home.



Hull or Cesena?

"I'd rather take the Hull job," Bent said.

"There's a chance to get into the Premier League next year. If a play-off team in the Championship can get into the Premier League, I'm going there."

4 Could Cole have kicked off his managerial career closer to home? Credit: Getty



Bent added: "Northern Italy sounds beautiful.

"But if he was to go to Hull, he's in the Premier League if they get promoted next year.

"Even if they don't get promoted, he's still in a very, very good league."

Although Hull stand a chance of returning to the English top flight for the first time since 2017, Cole could still feel the rush of promotion at Cesena.

How are Cesena performing this season?

Cesena sit 8th in Serie B, which would be good enough to secure a spot in the league's preliminary round for the promotion play-offs.

In Serie B, the top two teams in the 20-team division are automatically promoted to Serie A, with third and fourth progressing straight to the promotion play-offs semi-finals.



4 Cole has worked across several clubs and even for England under former interim boss Lee Carsley Credit: Getty

It means teams that finish 5th, 6th, 7th and 8th will have to take the longest route possible should they want to rub shoulders with the likes of Juventus and Inter Milan next season.

Teams in the Championship will soon become familiar with Serie B's play-off system as the EFL confirmed they will expand the play-offs from four teams to six, beginning with the 2026/27 campaign.

Although a surge to the top four of Serie B may be too difficult amid Cesena's surprising record, European football expert Andy Brassell believes the expectation for Cole will be to reach the play-offs.

Why Cole has a chance to 'make it his own'

"If you look at Cesena and where they are at the moment, they have a losing record," Brassell told talkSPORT.com.



"They've lost more games than they've won. So he's not expected to get into the top four. I think that would require an absolutely crazy run and a couple of teams above falling to bits.

"But it does mean that he needs to not just assure they make the top eight, but prep them for going into those playoffs.

4 Cole has been an assistant for five different teams but is finally making the step up Credit: Getty

"So it's quite a big job where he'll get a lot of time to work on the team and make it his own between now and the end of the season."

Despite the difficulty of the task that awaits, Brassell reserved special praise for Cole and his willingness to take the path less travelled to kick-start his managerial career.



"Look, Ashley Cole has proved so far by doing a variety of different jobs that he's a thoughtful coach," Brassell said.

"He's someone who's not turned his nose up at stuff that other big players would consider a bit menial. He's been really keen to learn the whole way.

"I think if you look at some people he knows, and I think you look at Cesc Fabregas and Dennis Wise, for example, there are people involved in Italian football who will speak for him, who know him. And I think that will have played a big part in him gettng involved there.

"But nevertheless, seeing a young English coach going there and having a go and trying to carve something out is really interesting."

Cole's first match in charge of Cesena is a trip to 15th-placed Mantova on Tuesday, with kick-off scheduled for 7pm.

News.title: Man, 64, charged with killing eight-year-old boy after he was blasted in head with shotgun during rabbit hunt
News.content:
GUNNED DOWN Man, 64, charged with killing eight-year-old boy after he was blasted in head with shotgun during rabbit hunt

A MAN has been charged with killing an eight-year-old boy who was shot in the head while on a rabbit hunt.

Jay Cartmell suffered fatal injuries to his head and face on a farm in Cumbria in September 2024.



The Crown Prosecution Service today confirmed Allan Thursby has now been charged with gross negligence manslaughter.



The 64-year-old will appear at Carlisle Magistrates’ Court on March 26.

Malcolm McHaffie, Head of the Crown Prosecution Service’s Special Crime Division, said: “Our prosecutors have worked to establish that there is sufficient evidence to bring the case to court and that it is in the public interest to pursue criminal proceedings.

“We have worked closely with Cumbria Police as they have carried out their investigation into the circumstances of this death. Our thoughts are with the family of Jay Cartmell at this time.”

An inquest heard previously how Jay was on a “rabbit shooting expedition” when he was suffered a shotgun injury.

The youngster was rushed to the Royal Victoria Infirmary in Newcastle by air ambulance but sadly could not be saved.

Paying tribute, Jay’s devastated parents Leigha and James Cartmell said their son enjoyed fishing and rabbiting and helped take care of the family pets.

They added: “We are heartbroken at the passing of our perfect little boy – Jay Cartmell, eight years.

“He was loving, kind and full of mischief, the best boy that anyone could wish for and the third corner of our beautiful family ‘triangle’.



“Jay loved being outdoors, the muddier he could get the better and was starting to follow in the footsteps of his dad with his obsession for speedway at Workington, where he first attended aged one years.”

News.title: Full list of 88 UK postcodes impacted today as Royal Mail delivery disruption continues
News.content:
DOZENS of postcodes across the UK have been hit by Royal Mail delivery delays.

A total of 88 areas in England, Scotland, Wales, and the North of Ireland have been affected by the disruption.



In its latest service update, Royal Mail revealed some areas won’t receive their mail on time.



The postal service has confirmed all the postcodes currently affected by delays.

It comes after Royal Mail was handed a £21 million fine in October, 2025, for missing first and second class delivery targets in its last financial year.

Findings showed that just 77% of first class mail and 92.5% of second class were delivered on time in its 2024-25 financial year, short of the service’s respective 93% and 98.5% targets.

“We aim to deliver to all addresses we have mail for, six days a week,” the delivery firm has stated.

“In a small number of local offices, this may temporarily not be possible due to local issues such as high levels of sick absence, resourcing, or other local factors.

“In those cases, we will rotate deliveries to minimise the delay to individual customers.”

Royal Mail added that it also provides “targeted support to those offices to address their challenges and restore our service to the high standard our customers would normally receive”.

It went on to apologise for “any inconvenience” and thanked customers their understanding.



While Royal Mail’s air and road networks operated as planned during the past 24 hours, the postal service confirmed not all mail was processed and dispatched as planned.

According to the service update, Plymouth Mail Centre is experiencing delays with some mail posted in the PL postcode area yesterday for delivery in other parts of the UK today.

As of Tuesday, March 17, Royal Mail has reported delays across 32 delivery offices, with a knock-on effect for 88 postcode areas.

The on-going delays come ahead of the hike in stamp prices next month, with First Class raised to £1.80 and Second Class increased to 91p.

News.title: New recycling bin rules for ALL households in England this April – or risk being slapped with £400 fine
News.content:
A NEW recycling rule is set to kick in at the end of this month – don’t follow it and you may be fined.

The new rule should make it easier for UK households to recycle.



From March 31, 2026, a huge change to recycling rules is coming to all councils.



Simpler Recycling is a scheme being launched to try and end the postcode lottery of bin collections across the country.

The new change will see you have four different containers – each intended to recycle a different material.

The first is for mixed recycling, including glass, cans and hard plastic.

Next is the paper and cardboard bin – this only applies to certain councils with the facilities to arrange a separate collection

The third addition is the food waste bin, which includes all raw or cooked food and even teabags.

General waste is the final bin – anything that cannot be recycled should be put here as normal.

As a part of the scheme, in March 2027, councils are expected to roll out soft plastic recycling – including plastic bags and film.

Fines for incorrect use of recycling bins can range in price, with the highest being £400.



Brits are advised to check with their local council on new bin rules if they have not already been notified.

News.title: How immensely fraught nuclear commando mission would end Iran war as Trump considers ‘biggest ever’ special forces raid
News.content:
DONALD Trump is weighing a do-or-die commando strike that could smash Iran’s nuclear threat once and for all.

The daring plan would see elite US and Israeli troops deep into hostile territory to seize – or destroy – Tehran’s hidden stockpile of enriched uranium.





Experts warn could become the biggest special forces raid in history.

And insiders say it’s a mission so dangerous it would make past operations look like warm-ups.



But with Iran still sitting on material that could fuel multiple nuclear bombs, the pressure is on to act now or risk catastrophe later.

Trump himself has made clear the option is firmly on the table.

He said: “They haven’t been able to get to it, and at some point, maybe we will.

“We haven’t gone after it, but it’s something we can do later on.”



Secretary of State Marco Rubio added bluntly: “People are going to have to go and get it.”

The target is Iran’s remaining uranium stockpile buried deep beneath heavily bombed nuclear sites in Isfahan, Fordow and Natanz.

Despite devastating US-Israeli airstrikes last year, hundreds of kilos of highly enriched uranium remain – enough to be turned into weapons-grade material in weeks.

That’s why Trump and Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu are now looking at the far more aggressive option of boots on the ground.

The goal would be to physically seize or neutralise the radioactive material, finishing the job airstrikes couldn’t.



But this is no quick in-and-out mission.

Experts say it could take days, involve hundreds or even over 1,000 troops, and require total military dominance in the skies and on the ground.

The operation could potentially include forces such as the 75th ranger regiment or the 82nd airborne division.

Retired US admiral James Stavridis warned it could mean “potentially the largest special forces operation in history”, according to the Wall Street Journal.

Unlike past lightning raids, this would be a brutal grind.



Commandos would have to smash into underground tunnel complexes, dig through rubble and debris, and hunt down uranium stored in sealed cylinders – all while avoiding booby traps and potential Iranian counter-attacks.

They would then face the added challenge of safely handling and transporting highly dangerous radioactive material, deep inside enemy territory.

François Diaz-Maurin, writing in the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists, told The Telegraph: “Logistically and tactically, this mission would be highly perilous, if not nearly impossible.”



The uranium itself adds another layer of danger.

Stored as uranium hexafluoride gas, it can turn into deadly toxic chemicals if exposed to moisture – meaning one mistake could trigger a lethal contamination event.

It comes as…

Cheryl Rofer, a former Los Alamos nuclear scientist, estimated the stockpile could be spread across dozens of cylinders, adding to the complexity, according to the Telegraph.

Even getting in and out is a nightmare.



Troops would likely fly in on MC-130J special ops aircraft or Chinook helicopters, backed by overwhelming US air power.

On the ground, elite units like Delta Force would be supported by larger formations such as Rangers or airborne troops to hold off Iranian forces.

Engineers, bomb disposal teams and nuclear specialists would all be needed.

And in case no runway is available, forces may have to build one on the spot, the Wall Street Journal reports.

Former Iran director at the National Security Council Richard Nephew added any operation would be “very large and very complicated.”



He told the WSJ: “I’m worried about the drone strikes, IED and similar traps, contamination risks, and the long time we’d need to have people on-site.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Joseph Rodgers, of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told The Telegraph: “Any operation to recover Iranian nuclear material comes with enormous risks.”

But he added: “Several weapons’ worth of highly enriched uranium cannot be left unattended in Iran.”

News.title: Dramatic photos show plumes of smoke over Midtown Manhattan as fire breaks out near NYC St. Patrick’s Day parade
News.content:
A FIRE broke out at a high-rise building in Manhattan Tuesday morning, near the route of the St. Patrick’s Day Parade.

The parade, which is expected to start around 11am Tuesday, brings in millions of onlookers.





Videos and photos of the scene show thick clouds of smoke billowing out of a building near Madison Avenue and East 43rd Street.

The New York City Fire Department (FDNY) told The U.S. Sun that there was a fire inside the HVAC system of a high-rise commercial building.



The blaze occurred at 6 East 43rd Street.

The parade’s start did not appear to be impacted as the FDNY shared photos of FDNY Emerald Society Pipes and Drums gearing up to march.

read more in news PARADE TRAGEDY Church-going mom of 2 crushed to death during St. Patrick’s Day parade

“It’s almost time,” the post read.

The smoke cleared shortly before 11am Tuesday but several fire trucks were still seen in the area.



A notice went out on X shortly after 10am telling locals to “expect traffic delays, road closures, mass transit disruptions and emergency personnel” in the area.

Locals were advised to use alternate routes.

The fire broke out near staging areas for the St. Patrick’s Day parade, which forms along Vanderbilt Avenue and nearby streets.

The parade then takes place with thousands marching up Fifth Avenue from East 43rd Street to East 79th Street.

Vanbarton Group was in the process of purchasing the 27-story building at 6 East 43rd Street for $140 million in May 2025, according to the Commercial Observer.



The deal closed at the end of September, with the real estate company announcing plans to convert the roughly 400,000-square-foot office building into 441 residential apartments.

Construction on the new apartments was set to start immediately. Vanbarton expects the apartments to be occupied by Spring 2027.

“The building will be transformed into a new residential tower that will provide much needed housing in a premier Midtown location, only steps from Grand Central Terminal and Bryant Park and at the center of New York City’s financial, legal, and corporate office hub,” Vanbarton Group said in a press release.



More to follow… For the latest news on this story, keep checking back at The U.S. Sun, your go-to destination for the best celebrity news, sports news, real-life stories, jaw-dropping pictures, and must-see videos.

News.title: Mum was hailed a ‘hero’ after husband & daughter died in fire… but fresh blaze exposed dark past & twisted incest secret
News.content:
FOR almost five years Carol Wray thought she had got away with a double murder.

Her husband Richard and disabled daughter Amanda died in a fire she had deliberately started at their council house in Halifax, West Yorkshire.





Carol posed as a victim and a hero and she even got a commendation from the coroner into the inquest into her husband and daughters’ deaths for supposedly saving her other children from the blaze.

And it wasn’t until five years later when Carol started yet another house fire that the authorities became suspicious and a murder investigation was launched.

Carol was implicated in FOUR serious house fires over the course of 13 years, which left two of her family members dead and others seriously injured.

She was eventually convicted of two counts of murder and three counts of arson and received FIVE life sentences.

Now a new documentary series , Britain’s Almost Perfect Murders, analyses Carol’s case for the first time and reveals how she too was a victim of the most heinous crimes which could have triggered her deadly firestarting .

Retired detective superintendent Trevor Wilkinson who eventually brought Wray to justice, says: “During the investigation it became clear that in 1985 she had committed the perfect murder. She had got away with it, she showed no remorse and it took five years for her criminality and her actions to come to light properly and for her to be sentenced for the crime that she did commit.”

Carol was one of seven children born in Hartlepool. But at the age of 11 Carol’s childhood was going to be changed forever – her father John Stoker started to sexually abuse her.

Carol tried to get away from her father and her family, and ran away to Halifax, West Yorkshire, where she got a job.



She was just 14 years old when she met Richard Wray, who was her supervisor at work.

Trevor Wilkinson says: “He was her childhood sweetheart. She was infatuated with him. She stole him from another woman, she saw him as the love of her life.”



By the age of 15 she fell pregnant and gave birth to their first child Mary in 1971. Son Shaun followed in 1973, followed by baby Amanda the following year.

However baby Amanda was born with physical disabilities which put a huge strain on Carol.

Trevor Wilkinson says: “The relationship deteriorated with his alcoholism, debt, he was terrible with money. But everyone thought he was a fun-loving person, and happy-go-lucky. Carol thought he was becoming evil, got them into debt, and he was unfaithful with other women.”

But despite her now being married and having children, Carol’s dad John John Stoker continued to sexually abuse her.

Criminologist Dr Jen Hough: “Now when someone is sexually abused at such a young age and so extensively, it often creates very problematic behaviours both in childhood and in adult life as well. You are not able to deal with things in a typical way, often you don’t have a healthy coping mechanism.”



In 1977 Carol became pregnant with her father’s child. She was forced to have an abortion to hide what he had been inflicting on her and her life began spiralling ever further into depression and out of control.

In December that year she set fire to the family home – but luckily no-one was injured although they had to move house.

Psychologist Dr Gareth Norris explains : “What we often see with females who have been abused by family members is they lack a sense of control in their lives and sometimes they will resort to problem behaviours to get that attention, get control of things and one of those is fire setting.

“Fire setting and arson are often seen as types of crimes that will attract a lot of attention, it is very destructive but there will also be fire engines, lots of people, a whole commotion that goes with it. And that to the person who is trying to get psychological impact from the event can be quite powerful.”



Richard and Carol got married in 1979, and Carol’s trauma continued as the children were taken into care, Richard lost his job causing him to drink even more heavily.

And the following year Carol’s sister Denise confided in her that she too had been abused by their father.

Dr Jen Hough says: “It’s not unusual that that abuse would then trauma bond someone like Wray to her dad. When Wray finds out that her sister is also being abused by her father, she isn’t able to cope with this, she becomes almost jealous of that.

“While to people who haven’t suffered that level of abuse that would seem absolutely outrageous, it is this trauma bonding that happens when somebody has been abused at such a young age and so significantly as well. That is where we see the fire-starting as a response to being unable to cope with stress in a normal healthy way.”

Fatal fire

In November 1980, Carol set her second house fire, and this time there were eight people, including herself in the house.



Trevor Wilkinson explains: “She set fire to the clothes in the airing cupboard and caused a major fire from which she went upstairs allegedly to warn her parents, her three brothers jumped out of the bedroom window and got seriously injured with broken bones, she jumped out and broke her arm.

“The firemen who attended the scene said it was like a battle scene. The house was totally gutted, but it was eventually, although it was held to be a bit suspicious, put down to an electrical fault.”

Carol was hospitalised for four weeks – she was safe and being cared for, and was free from the abuse. And then her children were returned to her.

But in 1985 Carol set fire to her family home – and this time it had fatal consequences.

Dr Jen Hough says: “Her husband spent long periods of time unemployed, he had an issue with alcohol and would often drink away what little money they did have as a family and she was trying to support three children within that environment as well.”



That night Richard got drunk on cider and passed out on the sofa while Carol put the children to bed.

Her anger was bubbling up and while her husband snored she struck a match and carefully paced it on the arm of a chair.

Very quickly the living room went up in flames. The TV exploded, the windows blew out. Carol screamed and woke her children. 14-year-old Mary grabbed her brother Shaun, 12, and dragged him to safety out of the front door. Amanda, 11, was so severely disabled that she wasn’t able to escape.

Trevor Wilkinson says: “Amanda was left in the house. Carol made no attempt to go back in and get Amanda or to help her husband out of the fire.”

Dr Jen Hough adds: “There was an eye-witness account that said Wray was just stood in the living room, motionless looking out to the world and it was only after a period of time that she tried to escape.”



The fire brigade came and found Richard dead from smoke inhalation. Amanda was in the bedroom with 35 per cent burns and was taken to hospital, but sadly she died 12 days later.

But Carol Wray convinced everybody that the fire had been started by a cigarette dropped by Richard when he was drunk.

And the inquest jury believed her recording an accidental death verdict. The coroner even commended her for rescuing her other two children.

And experts believe starting fires – despite the deadly consequences – had become a form of release from the trauma of her sexual abuse.



New fire

For the next five years Carol Wray essentially gets away with murder. It was as close to the perfect murder as you can imagine.

By 1990, Carol, Mary and Shaun were living in a new council house in nearby Rastrick. She was living with another woman who she had taken in as a lodger, and she had a young daughter.

Trevor Wilkinson says: “They had started a relationship and we could never get to the bottom of what the relationship was.”

Dawn Holroyd had a boyfriend and she went out on a date, leaving her infant daughter in Carol Wray’s care.

Trevor adds: “So Carol thought, if I start a small fire, she will come back home straight away. So she’s set fire to the curtains in the dining room. The fire got out of control, but she got the three children out of the house and the fire brigade came.”



By this point the association between Carol Wray and house fires was starting to ring alarm bells.

Trevor Wilkinson recalls: “One of the firemen who was there thought it was suspicious that he had been to a house on three occasions where there was a fire and Carol Wray was always present.”

Trevor launched an investigation. All three previous fires had been committed in different areas and therefore had been dealt with by different fire stations. So nobody had joined the dots.

Police arrested Carol Wray for the murder of her husband Richard and her daughter Amanda and with setting three other fires with intent to endanger life.

Trevor Wilkinson says: “We interviewed her but she didn’t confess when she first came in. But when she realised she wasn’t going to get away with it she used the defence that it was all because her father had been committing incest with her and was still committing incest with her up until two years before she was arrested.”



As a result of Carol’s revelation about the abuse she and her sister suffered, her dad John Stoker was arrested and charged with sexual assault.

He served ten years in prison.

Dr Jen Hough says: “When Carol Wray was finally arrested she seemed almost pleased and relieved to confess to the murders. She hadn’t meant to cause such harm. She may have felt it almost a cathartic release to confess.

“On the surface it might be very easy to say that Wray had psychopathic behaviours. But we need to remember the significant trauma she had faced throughout her life and the way that trauma manifests itself as people mature.

“This is not to excuse for what she has done but to explain why she has taken the unusual action of starting fires.



In December 1990 Carol Wray went on trial for two counts of murder and three counts of arson.

The jury found her guilty and the judge said she was a danger to the public with her persistence in lighting fires when she had a personal problem. She was given five life sentences.

Dr Jen Hough says: “It’s a case which could’ve been avoided. Carol Wray engaged with some very very unhealthy coping mechanism to deal with the trauma she had experienced.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“Had she had had more support, had she had had someone who cared for her, someone who didn’t abuse her in the way that her own father did, she might never have reached a point in her life where she set fires – ultimately fires which were lethal.”

Britain’s Almost Perfect Murders airs Thursdays at 10pm on the TRUE CRIME channel. Previous episodes are available to stream on WATCH FREE UK.

News.title: Popular dairy products stocked in major supermarket urgently recalled as chemical ‘unfit for human consumption’ found
News.content:
MILK and butter products have been urgently recalled after they were found to contain a harmful chemical.

The dairy produce, which was stocked in a major UK supermarket, were found to be “unfit for human consumption” due to the contamination.





Jersey Dairy confirmed last week that small batches of its 1L 2.5% fat reduced milk and 250g unsalted butter had been tainted.

The products were stocked in stores on the Channel Islands as well as Waitrose, which has since been removed them from shelves and marked both items as “out of stock”.

Now, the manufacturer has revealed the recall is the result of an “error” at one of its locations, which caused oil residue to enter the milking system while equipment was cleaned.

The mistake led to two Jersey Dairy products being “contaminated with a chemical unfit for human consumption”.



The details of the affected batches are as follows:

1L Fresh Jersey Milk 2.5% – use by date March 23, 2026

250g Genuine Jersey Unsalted Butter – best before date June 8, 2026

A statement from Jersey Dairy released on Saturday, March 14 reads: “It seems that an oil residue got into the system, which then contaminated the milk collected from the cows after that cleaning.

“The level of contamination was extremely low. Calculations estimate it to be below 1 part per million (ppm), which is below standard testing detection thresholds.

“As a result, these trace levels would not normally be identified through routine testing and quality control processes.”



The statement continued: “Jersey Dairy maintains stringent, high-level testing protocols and procedures to ensure the safety and quality of all our products.

“While this incident demonstrates that extremely low-level traces below detection limits can occur, it also shows that robust procedures are in place to respond quickly and responsibly when issues are identified.

“Once identified, the situation was contained and a precautionary recall of all potentially affected products was initiated.

“Most of the affected products have been now recovered and disposal of it is currently underway. Other Jersey Dairy products are unaffected.”

Jersey Dairy also emphasised that “consumer safety has always been our top priority” with managing director, Eamon Fenlon advising anyone in posession of the affected products to “bring it back to the shop and get a refund”.



There are currently no reports of illness and the level of contamination has been determined to be “very small” with ongoing investigations being carried out by the Environmental Health Department.

Jersey Dairy has confirmed the contamination has been “detained” and said the recall was issued as a “precautionary measure”.

Meanwhile, a statement from the Infrastructure & Environment Department Environmental and Consumer Protection Team determined “the risk to public health is considered to be low”.

In a statement to The Sun, spokesperson for Waitrose said: “In line with Jersey Dairy’s instructions, we have removed the two products from sale and asked any customers who purchased affected batches of this product, not to consume it, and to return it to one of our shops for a full refund.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Anyone with further concerns relating to this recall is advised to contact the Environmental and Consumer Protection Team via phone at 01534 445808 or email at environmentalhealth@gov.je.



The Sun has reached out to Jersey Dairy for comment.

News.title: Mum & her two young children died when ‘modified’ e-bike sparked horror blaze at family home that also left dad in coma
News.content:
BIKE TRAGEDY Mum & her two young children died when ‘modified’ e-bike sparked horror blaze at family home that also left dad in coma

A MUM and her two young children died when a “modified” e-bike sparked a horror blaze at the family home, an inquest heard.

Gemma Germeney, 31, Lilly Peden, eight, and Oliver Peden, four, all died in the fire in Cambridge on June 30, 2023.





The children’s dad, Scott Peden, was left in a coma for a month after the blaze, while the family’s two dogs also died.

At the time, the fire service said the most probable cause of the fire was an e-bike that was charging.

Cambridgeshire area coroner Elizabeth Gray told a pre-inquest review today: “I don’t think it’s disputed it was modified and caused the fire.”

Miles Bennett, counsel for Scott, asked for a person referred to in court only as Mr Severinov be considered as a witness and given interested person status in proceedings.



He told the court that “someone carried out a modification, quick fix, to that lithium battery”.

“Within a very short time after that quick fix was carried out there was a tumultuous fire at Mr Peden’s address,” the solictor added.

He said he found it “difficult to fathom that someone who may be responsible for carrying out” the modification may not be given interested person status.

Mr Bennett added: “It seemed to us that a person who may be central to how this happened should at the very least be a witness.”



He also told the court he has “no idea if there will be civil proceedings” by the OPSS (Office for Product Safety and Standard).

Alexandra Tampakopoulos, counsel for the OPSS, said: “If we have Mr Severinov giving evidence about the purchase of the battery and interactions with Mr Peden, it would be a little strange not to have Mr Peden give evidence.”

The coroner said that at the full inquest, background to the case would be outlined – including “the purchase of the bike, the battery being stolen, the modification”.

She adjourned proceedings, with a two-day full inquest hearing to take place on a date to be fixed.

Cambridgeshire Police said at the time that Gemma died at the scene, while her two children tragically passed away in hospital.



Following the horror, dad Scott called for urgent e-bike safety measures.

He told The Sun previously: “I still can’t process what’s happened. I lost my entire family, everything, in a matter of minutes.

“People shouldn’t be relaxed about charging batteries in their home during the summer. And there need to be stricter regulations over the resale of batteries – they should be checked and certified.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“We had smoke alarms, but because it was a lithium battery, we didn’t stand a chance.

“I lost everything in the fire, and I have to start all over again. The only positive to come from this is me making sure it does not happen to anyone else.”

News.title: Keir Starmer vows to 'weaken hand of Putin' as he embraces Zelensky
News.content:
Keir Starmer has pledged the UK will do all it can to "weaken the hand of Putin" during a meeting with Volodymyr Zelensky.

The PM welcomed the Ukrainian president to Downing Street on Tuesday, and reiterated the UK’s support for Kyiv, saying: “Our resolve is unbreakable.”

It came as the King earlier welcomed Mr Zelensky to Buckingham Palace as the Ukrainian president visits the UK to sign a new defence deal.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Image: PA) PA)

Speaking as the pair met in Downing Street, the PM said: "Let me start by paying tribute to the courage and resilience of the Ukrainian people over four years of this conflict, they've shown incredible courage, both on the front line and the civilians day in, day out. I hope that you can convey to them again that the UK stands with them for as long as it may take, our resolve is unbreakable, and will remain in that way.

"I think it's really important that we're clear that the focus must remain on Ukraine. There is obviously a conflict in Iran, going on in the Middle East, but we can't lose focus on what's going on in Ukraine and the need for our support there. Secondly, it's really important that we're clear and consistent that Putin can't be the one who benefits from the conflict in Iran, whether that's oil prices or the dropping of sanctions. It's really important that we keep up our resolve in relation to supporting Ukraine and doing everything we can to weaken the hand of Putin."

Mr Zelensky thanked the UK for its support over the winter. He said: “Thanks to the UK, you have stood with us all through this difficult winter.” The Ukrainian president said he would update The PM Keir on battlefield developments and energy security, saying: “Of course, we can’t sleep, we must move quickly.” He added: “Also not to forget about our diplomatic efforts and negotiations and where we are with the Americans and with the Russians.” Mr Zelensky also acknowledged the impact of the Iran war on Ukraine and Europe.

Earlier PM called for allies to “work in lockstep” to deliver global security after signing of a new defence deal between Britain and Ukraine. Downing Street said the partnership with Kyiv would bring together “Ukrainian expertise and the UK’s industrial base” to manufacture and supply drones and other capabilities. As part of the agreement, the UK will put £500,000 towards a new “AI centre of excellence” in Kyiv, which would be made up of experts working to see how the technology can best be used for a “battlefield advantage”, No 10 said.

The meeting comes at a time of heightened global instability in light of the Middle East conflict, during which US President Donald Trump has vented frustrations with the UK and other countries over the extent of their involvement, and the Ukraine war. The war in Iran has seen a rise in energy prices that has benefited the Russian despot Vladimir Putin despite worldwide sanctions. Last week the US temporarily announced it had eased sanctions preventing other countries buying Russian oil which was already loaded on vessels at sea.

News.title: Tenerife records 84 earthquakes in two days as officials launch 'plan for disaster'
News.content:
Tenerife records 84 earthquakes in two days as officials launch 'plan for disaster'

The popular Canary Islands holiday destination recorded 84 earthquakes over the weekend near Mount Teide, with officials starting work on a contingency plan in case of an eruption

View 4 Images Tenerife reported 84 earthquakes at the weekend (Image: Getty)

Tenerife experienced 84 earthquakes over the weekend, prompting officials to initiate a "plan for disaster". The National Geographic Institute (IGN) reported that the tremors were detected in the western part of Las Canadas on the island.

‌



Approximately 59 of these seismic events were accurately located, which they claim occurred around Mount Teide. The most notable activity consisted of two low-frequency pulses around the Canary Island.

‌



One happened between 1.30am and 5.30am on Saturday, with the second recorded between 7.30am and 10.30am on Sunday. It's believed they both occurred at depths of roughly eight and 21km below Las Canadas, each registering low magnitudes.

‌



However, the IGN has stated that these figures are provisional and could potentially rise. It stresses that this type of activity does not indicate an increased risk of a volcanic eruption in the forthcoming weeks or months, according to Canarias7.

This follows reports that the Santa Cruz de Tenerife Council has begun preparations on a contingency plan in the event of an eruption. Mount Teide, Spain's tallest peak, last erupted in 1909, reports the Express.

View 4 Images Teide is the highest peak in Spain (Image: Getty)

‌



A specialised technical department has now been set up to devise an action plan should it erupt again. The plan aims to bolster the city's readiness and ensure it can provide shelter and essential services.

It's believed the city could presently manage a "moderate emergency" but there are concerns a major eruption could pose more significant challenges. Santa Cruz Mayor Jose Manuel Bermudez stated: "We don't know how it will happen, how it will develop, or when it might occur.

"But scientists have indicated that current volcanic developments on the island are not something normal."

‌



The IGN monitors seismic activity in Spain through a network of over 100 stations, equipment and sampling points which are deployed throughout Tenerife. This enables continuous surveillance.

View 4 Images Tenerife has experienced 84 earthquakes in a weekend (Image: Getty)

Scientists can therefore conduct real-time monitoring to detect potential changes in seismic activity, ground deformations, or geochemistry. Tenerife, the largest of Spain's Canary Islands, is encircled by volcanic activity.

‌



Whilst the mayor has expressed concerns about preparedness to handle an eruption, the president of the island's governing council, the Cabildo of Tenerife, Rosa Davila, has previously dismissed speculation.

View 4 Images Mount Teide hasn't erupted since 1909 (Image: Getty)

Teide's 1909 eruption resulted in "minor damage" on the island. Yet an earlier eruption in 1706 is reported to have destroyed a town along with Tenerife's main port of Garachico.

Article continues below

"A month and a half ago this activity would not have attracted much attention," a spokesperson for the local National Geographic Institute said. "However, given the current context we will continue to monitor the situation closely in case anything changes."

News.title: Woman's sick lies after fatally poisoning husband then writing book about grief
News.content:
Woman's sick lies after fatally poisoning husband then writing book about grief

A mother-of-three wrote a book about grief following the sudden death of her husband. Then, she was found guilty of murdering him. The explosive trial exposed her sickening deceit

View 11 Images Kouri Richins has been found guilty of murdering husband by poisoning his Moscow Mule cocktail (Image: Facebook)

She fooled everyone with the grieving widow act - but it was all a ruse.

‌



Kouri Richins, a mum-of-three, has been found guilty of fatally poisoning her husband Eric Richins - in a cruel plot to cash in from his death. It took the jury just three hours to reach their verdict.

‌



It's an astonishing case that has gripped America. For a year after Eric's sudden shock death, his 35-year-old partner presented as a grieving widow. She even wrote a children's book - Are You With Me? - in an attempt to "try and understand not only how to grieve as a widow, as a wife, but also with my kids, how to help them, how to help them understand what just happened".

‌



She brazenly dedicated the book to her husband, calling him "my amazing husband and a wonderful father". But in May 2023, the whole pretence crumbled when she was arrested and charged with her husband's murder.

Richins remained largely stoic throughout her trial, but the facade cracked when she learned her fate. This week, she visibly gasped and hung her head as the judge informed her that the jury had also found her guilty on four other counts; aggravated attempted murder related to a previous murder plot, and two counts of insurance fraud and forgery.

Richins now faces 25 years to life in prison, with her sentencing scheduled for May 13 - what would have been Eric's 44th birthday. Here, we look at how the twisted mum's lies and how they unravelled...

‌



Twisted bride-to-be locked fiancé in cupboard and torched house before faking frantic 999 call READ MORE :

Eric's mysterious death

Businessman Eric was found dead inside the family home in Kamas, Utah, in the early hours of March 4, 2022. His wife had called 911 in the early hours of the morning, claiming she had returned to their shared room to find her husband cold in their bed.

She told authorities she had served her husband a mixed vodka drink in bed at around 9pm and then went to sleep with one of her young children who was having a nightmare. She later returned to her husband in their room around 3am where she said she found him "cold to the touch".

View 11 Images Kouri and Eric Richins with their three young sons (Image: Facebook)

‌



Richins- a realtor - told the dispatcher they had been celebrating her closing a deal on a $2.9 million mansion in Midway with some homemade Moscow Mule cocktails and a lemon drop shot.

But an autopsy found Eric died from a massive fentanyl overdose, with more than five times the lethal limit in his system.

Killer's first poison attempt

According to prosecutors, Richins' first plot to fatally poison Eric unfolded on Valentine's Day 2022 when she laced a sandwich she bought for her husband from a local diner.

‌



Eric fell ill and told friends he feared his wife was trying to poison him. Court documents show that between December 2021 and February 2022, his wife had texted someone asking for prescription pain medication. Three days after she obtained the drugs, she and her husband had a Valentine's Day dinner, after which he fell ill.

"Eric believed that he had been poisoned," the court documents said. "Eric told a friend that he thought his wife was trying to poison him."

View 11 Images Kouri Richins was also found guilty of attempted murder over the Valentine's Day incident (Image: Pool)

‌



Several witnesses gave testimony regarding the Valentine's Day incident - though their accounts varied.

That morning, Richins placed a to-go order with local restaurant Mirror Lake Diner for a Greek omelet and a hot bagel sandwich, which contained bacon, fried over hard egg, pepperjack cheese, avocado and tomato in a New York plain bagel, accompanied by hash browns. She collected the food at 9.07am, records showed, via Mail Online.

At 2.11pm, Eric called his close friend Josh Kaze. Kaze testified that it was "unlike any other conversation I'd had with Eric".

‌



Due to hearsay rules, Kaze could not testify as to exactly what Eric said, but noted that the call stood out to him because of both the topic of the conversation and Eric's emotions. Richins was found guilty of attempted murder over this incident.

Tidal wave of debt

Prosecutors told the jury that Richins was facing substantial debt and believed she would inherit millions from Eric's estate in the event of his death.

As a result of a pre-nuptial agreeement, she would have been limited in what she'd received in the event of a divorce, and the prosecutor argued that she wanted to end her marriage to Eric - but wanted his money.

‌



On the day of her husband's death, she owed more than $3.1 million related to her house-flipping business, court documents show. She also allegedly owed a massive $7.5 million in debt to more than 20 payday loan and high-interest lenders.

View 11 Images Kouri was said to be in huge debt at the time of her husband's death (Image: Facebook)

Just weeks before Eric died, she also took out a new life insurance policy on her husband - a handwriting expert testified it appeared to have been forged.

‌



However, unbeknownst to her, Eric - who owned a stone masonry business - had placed his money in a trust for his young sons and appointed his sister as the trustee. "The morning Eric died, she had a plan on how to spend Eric's money, not realising it was trust money," said Summit County prosecutor Brad Bloodworth

The lover

Money wasn't the only motivation for the killer mum, the prosecution argued. She was also planning a future with another man, whom she had been having an affair with.

Robert Josh Grossman took to the stand at her trial and told the jury that he began a romantic relationship with Richins in early 2020 after he moved to Utah for a job working with her. She allowed him to stay at a home she was flipping, he testified.

‌



View 11 Images Robert Josh Grossman told the jury that he began a romantic relationship with Kouri in early 2020

Prosecutors showed the jury texts between the former lovers. In one text exchange prior to Eric's death, Richins texted Robert saying, "If I was divorced right now and ask you to marry me tomorrow, you would?" "Yes. In Love with Y O U! Of course I would," he responded.

In another message, she told Robert: "If he could just go away and you could just be here! Life would be so perfect!!!"

‌



View 11 Images Text exchanges between the former lovers were shown to the court (Image: Summit County Court)

Jurors also heard that 16 days after Eric's death, Kouri sent her boyfriend a link to a Caribbean resort and wrote, "Are we there yet?" Then, about a month after the death, prosecutors said she texted Robert saying, "I think I want you to be my husband one day."

In a key moment during the trial, Robert broke down as he spoke about the woman he had once been 'head over heels' for. Wiping his eyes and resting his head down on the stand, he recalled a conversation he had with Richins just days after Eric died. She had asked him what it felt like to kill someone, he testified.

‌



The housekeeper

Much of the case hinged on testimony from the state's star witness, housekeeper Carmen Lauber, who had worked for the couple. She testified that she sold drugs to Richins four times around the time of Eric's death - including the fentanyl that was used to kill Eric.

Lauber's account about the drug purchases was supported by a tranche of mobile phone evidence which revealed a huge number of texts and calls between the two women in the dates surrounding the two reported attempts on Eric's life. In the three months before his death, they exchanged around 800 texts.

View 11 Images Housekeeper Carmen Lauber was the state's key witness (Image: Pool)

‌



Graphics shown to the jury also revealed distinct patterns of communication on certain days: Richins initiating contact with Lauber, Lauber contacting drug dealer Robert Crozier, Lauber and Kouri repeatedly communicating over the following few hours and Lauber and Crozier repeatedly communicating within the very same timeframe.

Prosecutors said that weeks earlier, on Valentine's Day, Kouri had tried to kill her husband by lacing his sandwich with fentanyl. He became extremely ill but recovered after using Benadryl and an EpiPen, charging documents said. Afterward, Richins asked Lauber for "something stronger," the housekeeper told prosecutors, specifically asking for "the Michael Jackson stuff".

Chilling internet searches

Richins tried to erase evidence from her phone, deleting her history of texts, images, calls and internet searches from the months leading up to and around the time of Eric's death. But a great deal of data was recovered - including evidence used to corroborate the testimony of the state's star witness, the housekeeper.

‌



View 11 Images Details of Kouri's internet search history were also revealed in court (Image: AP)

Richins' search history showed she searched "what is a lethal.dose.of.fetanayl (sic)," "luxury prisons for the rich America" and "if someone is poisned (sic) what does it go down on the death certificate as," per a digital forensic analyst, according to AP.

Also recovered were three GIFs, either sent from or received, on her device at around 8.30am on March 4 - five hours after she 'found' Eric - including a Trump meme with the slogan 'I'm rich', a person wiping their nose on money, and a meme that reads: 'Idiots. Idiots everywhere.'

‌



The book

About a year after Eric's death, Richins published 'Are You With Me?' to help her three boys process the loss, and even promoted it on a local news station. "I just wanted some story to read to my kids at night and I just could not find anything," she said in April 2023. "Just because he's not present here with us physically, that doesn't mean his presence isn't here with us. Dad is still here, it's just in a different way."

View 11 Images Kouri self-published a children's book titled 'Are You with Me?' following her husband's death (Image: Amazon)

Texts presented in court revealed that it was a ghostwriter - and not Richins - who had authored the book. One detective testified that a copy of the book was sent to the sheriff's office along with an anonymous note, which read: "There are two sides to every story. This is a true Kouri, a devoted wife and adoring mother. Thought you should know. From Anonymous."

‌



The package was traced to an Amazon account belonging to the killer's mother.

'Walk the dog' letter

While behind bars, Richins reportedly wrote a six-page letter to her mum Lisa Darden in which she made several claims - including that her brother Ronney would need to testify that Eric confided in him about buying fentanyl from Mexico.

The six-page document was found folded and tucked into a book in her cell in the Summit County jail. Prosecutors said it was an attempt to coach her family and friends on what to testify. The defense claimed it was part of a fiction book she was writing.

‌



At the top of the first page of the letter, she wrote, "Walk the dog!!! But take vague notes so you remember." The last line of the letter also states: "Take vague notes so you remember before you walk the dog."

View 11 Images Lisa Darden, Kouri Richins mother, listens to closing arguments of her daughter's trial (Image: AP)

Summit County Sheriff's Detective Jeff O’Driscoll read an excerpt to the jury. In it, Richins appears to give information to her mother that she wants her brother, Ronney Darden, to relay to her lawyer at the time, Skye Lazaro. (The firm where Lazaro works later withdrew from the case, citing an "irreconcilable" situation.)

‌



"Here is what I'm thinking but you have to talk to ronney. He would probably have to testify to this but its super short. He will need to tell Skye at the meeting next week."

She then began to describe an alleged conversation that she said Eric had with Ronney. "A year prior to Eric's death, Ronney was over watching football one Sunday and Eric and Ronney were chatting about Eric's Mexico trips. Eric told Ronney he gets pain pills and fentanyl from Mexico from the workers at the ranch," the letter read.

"There are pictures on my phone of Eric passed out on the floor or in the chair. Ronney should have texts from Eric talking about getting high as well… "Ronney never told me about the conversation. Eric finally told me and asked if Carmen could get him some," Richins said, referring to their housekeeper.

‌



View 11 Images The letter in question (Image: Summit County Court)

She then began to refer to herself in the third person. "Eric never wanted anyone to know he had an issue, especially get caught, he always wanted Kouri to go down for him," she continued. "When they traveled, Eric would put his drugs in Kouri's bag at the airlines right before they boarded. That way if they were ever caught, Kouri got in trouble, not him."

This would cause a "huge fight" because she would be angry that Eric would "risk her going to jail for his drug use". "He would just laugh about it," she said in the letter. "... Kouri has never taken any type of pills; didn't like them."

Article continues below

She added that Ronney should "reword this however he needs to make the point". "Just include it all," she wrote. "The connection has to be made with Mexico and drugs. Ronney will have the messages to prove Eric confided in him about getting high. He can be short and to the point but has to be done."

She further cautioned her mother to meet with her brother in person when she spoke with him about the letter, because she was worried her mother's home and phone may be "bugged".

Near the end of the excerpt, Richins tells her mother to "tell Ronney don't over analyze it". "Tell him I need him to do this," she added.

News.title: Meningitis vaccine update as some people may NOT be protected despite getting jab
News.content:
Meningitis vaccine update as some people may NOT be protected despite getting jab

At least two people have died and 13 others have been hospitalised following an outbreak that originated at a nightclub in Canterbury, Kent, health officials have said

View 3 Images Meningitis cases have been recorded among Kent students (Image: PA)

A meningitis vaccine update has made clear some people may not be protected despite getting the jab.

‌



The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) confirmed the strain of the disease that was identified in Kent is Meningitis B. It is the most common cause of meningitis in the UK.

‌



While routine vaccination against the B strain became routine for babies and young children from 2015, it was not standard when the latest group of students and those in their late teens were younger. Pupils aged between 13 and 14 are currently offered vaccinations against other strains of meningitis via school-run programmes.

‌



Tragically, two young adults died after the outbreak, appearing to stem from the Club Chemical nightclub in Canterbury. Sixth form pupil Juliette, 18, and an unnamed 21-year-old University of Kent student have tragically died while more than a dozen others were taken to hospital for treatment.

View 3 Images Students have queued up for antibiotics (Image: PA)

How to find out if your child has had the meningitis vaccine READ MORE :

Everything to know about the 7 common illnesses that could actually be meningitis READ MORE :

A total of 15 cases of meningitis have now been notified to the UKHSA with four being confirmed as menB infections. The government agency said in an update: "As of 5pm on 16 March 2026, UKHSA has been notified of 15 cases of invasive meningococcal disease with epidemiological links to Canterbury, Kent.

‌



"All cases have been hospitalised. Four of these cases have laboratory confirmation of meningococcal B infections. There have been two deaths."

Speaking in the House of Commons, UK Health Secretary Wes Streeting today said the risk to the wider population "remains low" but that the government is "actively contact tracing" those in close contact with people confirmed to have the illness.

He added: "If you're one of those students at the University of Kent who may have left campus... we're making arrangements for you to be able to visit your GP and receive your antibiotics there."

‌



View 3 Images Meningitis cases have been recorded in the South East (Image: Getty Images/Science Photo Library RF)

Trish Mannes, UKHSA Regional Deputy Director for the South East, added: "People who need antibiotic treatment are responding to our calls to come forward and we are working closely with the NHS to ensure these are offered at the various sites throughout today."

"Once again we remind anyone who visited Club Chemistry between 5th and 7th March to come forward for preventative antibiotic treatment as a precaution, as well as those offered antibiotics at the university.

Article continues below

"Investigations have now confirmed that some of the cases are Men B. It is important to be aware that the MenACWY vaccine routinely offered to teenagers does not protect against Men B, which is why knowing the symptoms and seeking early treatment is so important."

"If you think you may have symptoms of meningitis, don't hesitate to seek medical help by contacting your GP or calling NHS 111."

News.title: Two trapped in Cornwall as yacht bursts into flames in 'catastrophic' incident
News.content:
Two trapped in Cornwall as yacht bursts into flames in 'catastrophic' incident

Rescue crews were called out after the yacht suffered 'catastrophic' engine failure and caught fire off the coast of Fowey in south Cornwall - with two people trapped on board

View 2 Images RNLI crews were called after the yacht suffered catastrophic engine failure (Image: Loop Images/Universal Images Group via Getty Images)

Two people were left stranded onboard a 14-metre sailing yacht after its engine burst into flames off the coast of a picturesque harbour town.

‌



RNLI crews were called out just before 10.30am on Saturday, to waters off the coast of Fowey, south Cornwall, following reports that the vessel had suffered "catastrophic" engine failure which then ignited. At the time of the incident, the yacht was around "12 nautical miles south southeast of Fowey" with both individuals still trapped on board.

‌



A Border Force and a Royal Fleet Auxiliary (RFA) vessel were deployed to assist the RNLI. When rescue crews arrived, the fire was confirmed to have been extinguished - but they still boarded the yacht to assess its condition.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 2 Images Rescue crews encountered a 'significant hailstorm' (Image: RNLI/Neil Humphrey)

‌



It is not believed that either person on board suffered any injuries, reports Cornwall Live.

Rescue teams attached the yacht to a line before a lifeboat began to tow it back to shore - but the group then encountered a "significant hailstorm" on the way. After making it safely through the storm, the yacht was towed into Fowey Harbour and the crews were stood down.

In a statement the RNLI said: "Falmouth Coastguard requested the launch of our all-weather Severn class lifeboat Sybil Mullen Glover to locate, assess and assist a 14m sailing vessel with two people on board following an engine fire 12 nautical miles south southeast of Fowey.

Article continues below

"It was soon confirmed that the vessel had suffered catastrophic engine failure and that any fire had been extinguished. [..] A tow line was rigged and the vessel was taken in tow towards the nearest safe haven."

News.title: 'I was 26 and told I was healthy - then they said I could have days to live'
News.content:
'I was 26 and told I was healthy - then they said I could have days to live'

Luke Taylor, 27, says he was told his headaches and sickness were 'just migraines'

View 4 Images Luke Taylor in hospital (Image: Nia Jones/SWNS)

A father was told he 'could have days to live' after previously being told he was 'healthy'. Luke Taylor, 27, had been enduring excruciating pain in his head for 10 years and would also experience nausea, vomiting for days at a time.

‌



He said he repeatedly visited his GP and claims he was told he was "healthy" and his symptoms were "just migraines". However, in July 2025 his symptoms deteriorated significantly, when he began being sick and suffering from migraines several times a day.

‌



He insisted on an MRI scan and the results revealed that Luke had a haemangioblastoma - an extremely rare brain tumour that can develop throughout the brain and spinal cord. Luke underwent a nine-hour brain operation to remove the tumour at Walton Hospital, Liverpool, and was told surgeons were able to excise all of it. Now Luke will undergo scans on his eye, brain, spine and body every six months for the next decade to monitor any recurrence.

‌



Luke, a civil engineer, from Warrington, Cheshire, said: "When you get news like that, you go into yourself. Your mind races, you don't want to speak to anyone, you think your life is done.

"I was 26 when I was diagnosed. I had the rest of my life ahead of me, I have a daughter, and I wasn't sure how I was going to tell my family.

"They said if I didn't have emergency surgery, I could have days to live. I thought I was going to die."

‌



View 4 Images Luke Taylor, 27, had been suffering from horrendous pain in his head for a decade and would also experience sickness (Image: Nia Jones/SWNS)

After enduring migraines for 10 years, Luke visited his GP requesting an MRI scan. At first, Luke maintains, medics informed him it wasn't required, but he pushed back and arranged a scan at Warrington Hospital.

"One day, I was fed up with my headaches - they were getting worse and worse - I wanted to cry with the pain," he said. "I went to the doctors and they told me I was having really bad migraines, but that didn't make sense. I was being sick, I was in severe pain, and I said I wanted to have an MRI scan."

‌



In July 2025, Luke received a diagnosis of haemangioblastoma. By the point of discovery, the tumour had expanded to such an extent - larger than a golf ball - that Luke was informed he had merely days to survive without urgent surgical intervention.

Within seven days, he went through a nine-hour operation at Walton Hospital to extract the tumour. Regrettably, complications arose resulting in a brain haemorrhage, necessitating additional emergency surgery the next day.

Luke's partner, Nia Jones, 26, a business operations specialist, said: "I saw Luke after his operation - he was very erratic. He was slurring his words and couldn't open his eyes. Then a day after the surgery, we were told he had a bleed on the brain and had to go back in for emergency surgery."

‌



View 4 Images Nia Jones and Luke Taylor (Image: Nia Jones/SWNS)

Following 18 days in hospital, Luke was released and faced the challenge of relearning basic functions including walking, speaking and hand coordination after the dual operations. Nia revealed: "What followed were some of the hardest months. Luke had to relearn how to walk, speak again and use his hands.

"He couldn't dress himself or even cut up his own food. Every day was frustrating, exhausting and emotional - for both of us - but we were determined to do everything possible to help him recover."

‌



In November 2025, Luke was informed that the operation had been successful and doctors had managed to remove the entire tumour. He will now have scans on his eye, brain, spine and body every six months for the next decade.

"I was overwhelmed when they told me the surgery was successful," Luke admitted. "I cried my eyes out, I was so happy they got it all."

View 4 Images Luke and Nia will raise money for charity this summer (Image: Nia Jones/SWNS)

‌



On July 18, 2026, Nia, Luke, his sister Leah, and her partner Gary, plan to hike the three Yorkshire peaks in a single day to raise funds for the Brain Tumour Charity.

Nia said: "We have gone through a lot with the family. We wanted to raise money for the charity that has supported us throughout and show support to others. We want to show people that there is light at the end of the tunnel and people can get through this."

Joanna Moss, director of services at The Brain Tumour Charity, said: "Faster diagnosis of brain tumours is vital. That's why The Brain Tumour Charity works with healthcare professionals to increase their confidence in recognising signs and symptoms, and in referring for scans if they are at all concerned."

Article continues below

Donations can be made via Nia's JustGiving page.

News.title: Two men 'repeatedly raped woman on beach as third filmed' – she 'heard laughter'
News.content:
Two men repeatedly raped a woman on Brighton beach while a third one filmed the incident, jurors have been told.

Karin Al-Danasurt, 20, Ibrahim Alshafe, 25, and Abdulla Ahmadi, 26, allegedly targeted the woman in the early hours of October 4 last year on Brighton beach, after she became separated from her friends while on a night out. Al-Danasurt is also accused of having filmed the alleged rapes, later sending the recordings to Ahmadi's phone, a trial at Hove Crown Court heard on Tuesday.

Prosecutor Hanna Llewellyn-Waters told jurors the complainant was intoxicated at the time of the alleged rapes and "to all intents and purposes, incapacitated". Ms Llewellyn-Waters told the court: "She was repeatedly abused for their sexual gratification and entertainment. They wanted sex and that could be achieved by being with someone who was in no state to resist them."

The alleged victim was "staggering in the street" alone when she was approached by the three defendants, the court heard. "Instead of offering her help or even just leaving her be, these defendants targeted her," Ms Llewellyn-Waters said. "That targeting was not founded in good will or bonhomie, in high spirits - it was cynical, predatory and callous. Al-Danasurt himself in his police interview recognised that she was unable to even stand without support," the prosecutor added.

Image: EDDIE MITCHELL) EDDIE MITCHELL)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Alshafe and Ahmadi took the complainant to a location behind a beach shack which obscured the view on to the beach and raped the woman "repeatedly", jurors were told. "Mr Al-Danasurt went on to that beach moments after his friends took the complainant to the location," Ms Llewellyn-Waters said.

Although the complainant could not be definitive as to whether she was also raped by Al-Danasurt, the prosecution alleges Al-Danasurt was present and “fully aware of what was happening”.

"His presence and actions served to encourage the commission of those rapes by his co-defendants," Ms Llewellyn-Waters told jurors. "Again, footage retrieved from his telephone shows that he filmed the complainant being raped by Alshafe and Ahmadi."

Jurors were told they would be shown the footage during the course of the trial. The woman told police she recalled being spat on, kicked and her throat being grabbed during the alleged rapes, as well as men laughing, Ms Llewellyn-Waters told jurors.

After the alleged rapes, the complainant "crawled off the beach" and was captured on CCTV coming up the ramp to the esplanade alongside Alshafe, the prosecutor added. "Google translate entered into his telephone within a couple of minutes of them coming into shot details him telling her to 'unlock it', presumably her phone, and, far from wanting to give him her number, she told him to ‘f*** off’," Ms Llewellyn-Waters told jurors.

At the time of the alleged offences, all three defendants knew each other and were residents at the Cisswood House Hotel in Horsham, West Sussex, which was Home Office-approved accommodation for asylum seekers, jurors were told.

Ahmadi, an Iranian national living in Crewe in Cheshire, and Alshafe, an Egyptian national living in Horsham, have each denied two counts of raping the woman.

Al-Danasurt, an Egyptian national living in Horsham, is jointly charged on all four counts of rape as a secondary party "encouraging the rape by his actions at the scene, including filming it", and has pleaded not guilty to all four. He denies a fifth count of "sharing intimate films" without the complainant’s consent. The trial continues.

News.title: Airline worker had boob job while 'helping to smuggle millions of pounds to Dubai'
News.content:
Airline worker had boob job while 'helping to smuggle millions of pounds to Dubai'

Emma Rauf, who also allegedly bought designer goods, was suspected of stealing hundreds of thousands from the gangsters and shots were later fired into the door and window of her family home, her trial is told

View 5 Images Emma Rauf denies money laundering offences (Image: Handout)

An airline worker bought designer goods and a boob job while helping a gang smuggle millions of pounds of cash to Dubai, a court heard.

Emma Rauf, 33, is alleged to have waived excess baggage fees for couriers who trafficked between £14million and £30million in used bank notes hidden in suitcases. Rauf is said to have abused her position at a check-in desk at Manchester Airport for couriers who were smuggling the cash to the UAE city.

Her parents' home was later shot at after £406,500 was stolen from her car after she left it in the boot on her drive overnight, Bolton Crown Court was told.

The gangster who owned the missing cash warned that whoever took it would get a "bullet through the head" and ordered Rauf to take a lie detector test, the court heard. After Rauf refused, several bullets were fired through the front door and lounge window of the family home in Hale.

View 5 Images A Rolex watch was shown to the jury (Image: Handout)

She was later seen taking full bags to a safety deposit box in Manchester City Centre, the court heard. The jury watched videos allegedly sent by Rauf to relatives showing off a Louis Vuitton handbag, Valentino shoes, a diamond ring and Rolex watch inside a Bentley.

The court also heard messages that showed Rauf allegedly had cosmetic breast surgery paid for due to her role in the crime.

View 5 Images A diamond ring featured in the video (Image: Handout)

Bill Baker KC, prosecuting, said she was prepared to risk her job at the Emirates airline to "enjoy the lifestyle" that was brought by commissions on the laundered cash. He said: "The lure of designer goods and expensive watches was no doubt her motivation for involving herself in very serious organised crime."

But the court heard the operation also brought major risks to her family after some of the money went missing while under her control, Mr Baker said. Rauf's brother Ben Royle, 31, formerly of Wilmslow, and his friend Sheikh Jobe, 33, from Manchester, earlier pleaded guilty to money laundering for their roles in the smuggling and are due to be sentenced after the trial.

View 5 Images Luxury shoes said to have been bought by the defendant (Image: Handout)

The court heard that Royle's partner Phoebe Adamson, who is not implicated in the offending, was left terrified after three men in ski masks tried to break into his Manchester flat while she was alone in it after he stored £500,000 in cash there.

Rauf's parents' home was later shot at after she lost £406,500, that she and Royle earlier collected from criminals. Mr Baker said the couriers failed to declare any of the cash at Manchester and Birmingham airports, despite a legal requirement to do so for sums of over 10,000 Euros.

View 5 Images A designer bag shown to the jury (Image: Handout)

Some of the money, which varied from about £100,000 to £667,949 was declared upon arrival in Dubai where there are no checks as to the origins of the money,before it was paid into local bank accounts to launder the cash.

Mr Baker told the court that the total amount of declarations made to Dubai customs by the couriers was just under £14 million over more than 135 return flights. He said: "In fact they probably took out up to £30m as just over half was not declared and on non declared flights, they still took suitcases and phone messages showed there was money but some of it was not declared."

The alleged ringleader, who cannot be named for legal reasons, is now believed to be in Dubai after he failed to attend for the trial, the court heard. The money involved came from major organised criminals involved in drug supply in the north of England and the smuggling placed the couriers involved at risk of serious reprisals if any went missing, the court heard.

Rauf, of Wilmslow, denies money laundering offences.

Article continues below

The trial continues.

Boxing.title: Jake Paul suffers embarrassing hygiene gaffe during Donald Trump rally appearance as major wardrobe error exposes long-held insecurity
Boxing.content:
YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump at a political rally.

Paul joined Trump on stage at Verst Logistics in Kentucky and was given the chance to address the crowd.

The 29-year-old went viral on social media, not for his comments about Trump and American manufacturing, but instead about his appearance.

Many pointed out vast sweat patches that were visible on Paul's beige suit as he spoke to the crowd.

The patches were particularly prominent under his armpits, which were exposed further when he raised his arms to the crowd.

Paul had previously spoken about how sweating had long been a major insecurity of his.

Jake Paul gets on stage at the Trump rally and has the nastiest pit stains I've ever seen



(it also appears the crowd doesn't know who he is) pic.twitter.com/3Yc071tZzf — Aaron Rupar (@atrupar) March 11, 2026

YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump

Paul and his brother Logan had promoted his deodorant brand ahead of his fight with Mike Tyson back in 2024

Paul made the revelation back in 2024 when he launched a deodorant brand W, which he and his brother Logan promoted before and after his bout with Mike Tyson.

'Sweating has been a longtime insecurity of mine,' Paul had said when launching the brand.

'If you know me, you know I'm a clean freak. I always want to smell fresh, but training in Puerto Rico's humid climate makes it a real challenge.

'I needed a product that could keep up with my lifestyle — one that smells amazing but isn't full of junk ingredients, and honestly, one that wasn't from 2006.

'That's why I started W… to create products for guys that will boost their confidence and smell amazing, but also don't cost $20 for a stick of deodorant.'

The W deodorants are claimed to offer 24 hours protection, with the brand website stating: 'With doubled scent load, increased hydration and more sweat-blocking power, our new deodorant and body wash are a monumental breakthrough for mankind (or at least for your morning)'.

During the event, Trump signalled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

Join the debate Did YOU spot Jake Paul's hygiene gaffe?

Trump had brought Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday in Kentucky

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

'And you have my complete and total endorsement.'

Paul had expressed his admiration for Trump credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul added. And I know [Trump's] going to be the one to bring that here to us.

'I know God is with us. I know he wants us on the right side of history, and everyone here has to do their part.

'And God's got us. Trump's got us. God bless.'

Trump returned the praise, telling the crowd: 'He's a great guy. He's a courageous guy and a talented guy. He's a hell of a fighter, too, by the way.'



The endorsement of Paul's fighting spirit comes as the 29-year-old continues to dominate headlines following his endeavours in the boxing ring over recent years.

Boxing.title: Donald Trump gives Jake Paul shock endorsement for political career as boxer joins President at Kentucky rally
Boxing.content:
President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday and made a bold prediction about the social media star's future.

The 29-year-old Ohio native, who has transitioned from a YouTube personality to a major force in the combat sports world, stood alongside the president as the crowd in Hebron, Ky roared in support.

During the event, Trump signaled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

The president didn't stop at a mere prediction, immediately offering his political backing to the 'Problem Child' should he decide to pursue a seat in the future.
